title: The Vision Of Sin
content:
I.

I had a vision when the night was late:
A youth came riding toward a palace-gate.
He rode a horse with wings, that would have flown,
But that his heavy rider kept him down.
And from the palace came a child of sin,
And took him by the curls, and led him in,
Where sat a company with heated eyes,
Expecting when a fountain should arise:
A sleepy light upon their brows and lipsï¿½
As when the sun, a crescent of eclipse,
Dreams over lake and lawn, and isles and capesï¿½
Suffused them, sitting, lying, languid shapes,
By heaps of gourds, and skins of wine, and piles of grapes.


II.

Then methought I heard a mellow sound,
Gathering up from all the lower ground;
Narrowing in to where they sat assembled
Low voluptuous music winding trembled,
Wovï¿½n in circles: they that heard it sighï¿½d,
Panted hand-in-hand with faces pale,
Swung themselves, and in low tones replied;
Till the fountain spouted, showering wide
Sleet of diamond-drift and pearly hail;
Then the music touchï¿½d the gates and died;
Rose again from where it seemï¿½d to fail,
Stormï¿½d in orbs of song, a growing gale;
Till thronging in and in, to where they waited,
As ï¿½twere a hundred-throated nightingale,
The strong tempestuous treble throbbï¿½d and palpitated;
Ran into its giddiest whirl of sound,
Caught the sparkles, and in circles,
Purple gauzes, golden hazes, liquid mazes,
Flung the torrent rainbow round:
Then they started from their places,
Moved with violence, changed in hue,
Caught each other with wild grimaces,
Half-invisible to the view,
Wheeling with precipitate paces
To the melody, till they flew,
Hair, and eyes, and limbs, and faces,
Twisted hard in fierce embraces,
Like to Furies, like to Graces,
Dashï¿½d together in blinding dew:
Till, killï¿½d with some luxurious agony,
The nerve-dissolving melody
Flutterï¿½d headlong from the sky.


III.

And then I lookï¿½d up toward a mountain-tract,
That girt the region with high cliff and lawn:
I saw that every morning, far withdrawn
Beyond the darkness and the cataract,
God made Himself an awful rose of dawn,
Unheeded: and detaching, fold by fold,
From those still heights, and, slowly drawing near,
A vapour heavy, hueless, formless, cold,
Came floating on for many a month and year,
Unheeded: and I thought I would have spoken,
And warnï¿½d that madman ere it grew too late:
But, as in dreams, I could not. Mine was broken,
When that cold vapour touchï¿½d the palace gate,
And linkï¿½d again. I saw within my head
A gray and gap-toothï¿½d man as lean as death,
Who slowly rode across a witherï¿½d heath,
And lighted at a ruinï¿½d inn, and said:


IV.

ï¿½Wrinkled ostler, grim and thin!
Here is custom come your way;
Take my brute, and lead him in,
Stuff his ribs with mouldy hay.

ï¿½Bitter barmaid, waning fast!
See that sheets are on my bed;
What! the flower of life is past:
It is long before you wed.

ï¿½Slip-shod waiter, lank and sour,
At the Dragon on the heath!
Let us have a quiet hour,
Let us hob-and-nob with Death.

ï¿½I am old, but let me drink;
Bring me spices, bring me wine;
I remember, when I think,
That my youth was half divine.

ï¿½Wine is good for shrivellï¿½d lips,
When a blanket wraps the day,
When the rotten woodland drips,
And the leaf is stampï¿½d in clay.

ï¿½Sit thee down, and have no shame,
Cheek by jowl, and knee by knee:
What care I for any name?
What for order or degree?

ï¿½Let me screw thee up a peg:
Let me loose thy tongue with wine:
Callest thou that thing a leg?
Which is thinnest? thine or mine?

ï¿½Thou shalt not be saved by works:
Thou hast been a sinner too:
Ruinï¿½d trunks on witherï¿½d forks,
Empty scarecrows, I and you!

ï¿½Fill the cup, and fill the can:
Have a rouse before the morn:
Every moment dies a man,
Every moment one is born.

ï¿½We are men of ruinï¿½d blood;
Therefore comes it we are wise.
Fish are we that love the mud,
Rising to no fancy-flies.

ï¿½Name and fame! to fly sublime
Throï¿½ the courts, the camps, the schools,
Is to be the ball of Time,
Bandied by the hands of fools.

ï¿½Friendship!ï¿½to be two in oneï¿½
Let the canting liar pack!
Well I know, when I am gone,
How she mouths behind my back.

ï¿½Virtue!ï¿½to be good and justï¿½
Every heart, when sifted well,
Is a clot of warmer dust,
Mixï¿½d with cunning sparks of hell.

ï¿½O! we two as well can look
Whited thought and cleanly life
As the priest, above his book
Leering at his neighbourï¿½s wife.

ï¿½Fill the cup, and fill the can:
Have a rouse before the morn:
Every moment dies a man,
Every moment one is born.

ï¿½Drink, and let the parties rave:
They are fillï¿½d with idle spleen;
Rising, falling, like a wave,
For they know not what they mean.

ï¿½He that roars for liberty
Faster binds a tyrantï¿½s power;
And the tyrantï¿½s cruel glee
Forces on the freer hour.

ï¿½Fill the can, and fill the cup:
All the windy ways of men
Are but dust that rises up,
And is lightly laid again.

ï¿½Greet her with applausive breath,
Freedom, gaily doth she tread;
In her right a civic wreath,
In her left a human head.

ï¿½No, I love not what is new;
She is of an ancient house:
And I think we know the hue
Of that cap upon her brows.

ï¿½Let her go! her thirst she slakes
Where the bloody conduit runs,
Then her sweetest meal she makes
On the first-born of her sons.

ï¿½Drink to lofty hopes that coolï¿½
Visions of a perfect State:
Drink we, last, the public fool,
Frantic love and frantic hate.

ï¿½Chant me now some wicked stave,
Till thy drooping courage rise,
And the glow-worm of the grave
Glimmer in thy rheumy eyes.

ï¿½Fear not thou to loose thy tongue;
Set thy hoary fancies free;
What is loathsome to the young
Savours well to thee and me.

ï¿½Change, reverting to the years,
When thy nerves could understand
What there is in loving tears,
And the warmth of hand in hand.

ï¿½Tell me tales of thy first loveï¿½
April hopes, the fools of chance;
Till the graves begin to move,
And the dead begin to dance.

ï¿½Fill the can, and fill the cup:
All the windy ways of men
Are but dust that rises up,
And is lightly laid again.

ï¿½Trooping from their mouldy dens
The chap-fallen circle spreads:
Welcome, fellow-citizens,
Hollow hearts and empty heads!

ï¿½You are bones, and what of that?
Every face, however full,
Padded round with flesh and fat,
Is but modellï¿½d on a skull.

ï¿½Death is king, and Vivat Rex!
Tread a measure on the stones,
Madamï¿½if I know your sex,
From the fashion of your bones.

ï¿½No, I cannot praise the fire
In your eyeï¿½nor yet your lip:
All the more do I admire
Joints of cunning workmanship.

ï¿½Lo! Godï¿½s likenessï¿½the ground-planï¿½
Neither modellï¿½d, glazed, nor framed:
Buss me, thou rough sketch of man,
Far too naked to he shamed!

ï¿½Drink to Fortune, drink to Chance,
While we keep a little breath!
Drink to heavy Ignorance!
Hob-and-nob with brother Death!

ï¿½Thou art mazed, the night is long,
And the longer night is near:
What! I am not all as wrong
As a bitter jest is dear.

ï¿½Youthful hopes, by scores, to all,
When the locks are crisp and curlï¿½d;
Unto me my maudlin gall
And my mockeries of the world.

ï¿½Fill the cup, and fill the can:
Mingle madness, mingle scorn!
Dregs of life, and lees of man:
Yet we will not die forlorn.ï¿½


V.

The voice grew faint: there came a further change:
Once more uprose the mystic mountain-range:
Below were men and horses pierced with worms,
And slowly quickening into lower forms;
By shards and scurf of salt, and scum of dross,
Old plash of rains, and refuse patchï¿½d with moss.
Then some one spake: ï¿½Behold! it was a crime
Of sense avenged by sense that wore with time.ï¿½
Another said: ï¿½The crime of sense became
The crime of malice, and is equal blame.ï¿½
And one: ï¿½He had not wholly quenchï¿½d his power;
A little grain of conscience made him sour.ï¿½
At last I heard a voice upon the slope
Cry to the summit, ï¿½Is there any hope?ï¿½
To which an answer pealï¿½d from that high land,
But in a tongue no man could understand;
And on the glimmering limit far withdrawn
God made Himself an awful rose of dawn.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
